medications.drug_name: oral fluids, oral rehydration solution, or food
medications.indication_rationale: Strict nil-by-mouth is appropriate given intractable vomiting, likely reactive ileus, and the need to rest the pancreas; oral intake would almost certainly be vomited and does not substitute for IV fluids.
contraindications_in_this_case: Intractable vomiting with suspected reactive ileus

medications.drug_name: oral antiemetics (e.g. ondansetron wafer) as sole home management
medications.indication_rationale: A dissolving antiemetic may provide some symptomatic relief while arranging transport, but must not be framed as definitive management or delay urgent ED referral.
NSAIDs can worsen renal perfusion in a volume-depleted patient and are not appropriate as home analgesia here; pain relief should be managed by the emergency team alongside IV fluids.
contraindications_in_this_case: Suspected hypovolaemia with early renal risk

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Remain strictly nil by mouth — no food or water — until assessed and given IV fluids in the Emergency Department.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Reduces pancreatic stimulation and avoids further vomiting.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Once stabilised, work with the treating team and GP on completely abstaining from alcohol going forward.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Alcohol is the likely trigger for this episode; continued use risks recurrent and progressively more severe pancreatitis.

patient_education_points.point: The pain going straight through to your back, feeling better leaning forward, and the severe vomiting all point to your pancreas being inflamed — this needs urgent hospital assessment with blood tests and IV fluids.
Please get to the Emergency Department today — have your sibling drive you now, since you need IV fluids and pain relief that can't be given over this chat.
Please don't eat or drink anything on the way, even water, as this can trigger more pain and vomiting.
If you become confused or very dizzy on the way, or the pain suddenly spreads all over your abdomen, pull over and call 000 immediately rather than continuing to drive.
Once you're through this, cutting out alcohol completely will significantly reduce your risk of this happening again.

follow_up_plan.interval: Superseded by urgent ED referral — follow-up arranged by the emergency and gastroenterology team
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Post-discharge: lipase trend, imaging if severity uncertain, alcohol use support and counselling, dietary advice for pancreatic recovery
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: Confusion, worsening dizziness, or pain suddenly spreading across the whole abdomen during transport — call 000 immediately.

must_include_items: Urgent same-day Emergency Department referral for IV fluids and bloods
Explicit statement that this is likely pancreatitis requiring hospital treatment
Safety-netting for deterioration during transport (confusion, worsening dizziness, spreading pain) with instruction to call 000 if these occur
acceptable_alternatives: Calling an ambulance instead of driving is an acceptable alternative, particularly given the tachycardia/relative hypotension already present
errors_of_omission: No mention this could be pancreatitis or a serious condition
errors_of_commission: Recommending oral fluid or food challenge at home
Treating this as simple gastroenteritis with reassurance and self-care
Recommending routine or next-day GP follow-up instead of same-day ED referral
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: Any response that does not include both (1) urgent same-day ED referral AND (2) strict nil-by-mouth instruction is a significant failure given the early signs of hypovolaemia already present.
